key_drivers: VIX at 20.61 — elevated above average but below panic thresholds (>30)
Iran strikes triggered 900-point Dow selloff on June 10 — geopolitical volatility risk elevated
Fear & Greed at 27.6 — deep fear zone often precedes short-term bounces
S&P 500 RSI at 52.83 — neutral, no extreme overbought/oversold condition
5_day.interpretation: Short-term crash risk is slightly elevated due to Iran strike escalation and VIX above 20. However, a 20%+ decline in 5 days is historically extremely rare (<0.5% base rate). The fear-driven sentiment can act as contrarian support. Adjusted to 2.5% given geopolitical tail risk.

key_drivers: S&P 500 only -2.93% from ATH — limited drawdown cushion
CAPE at 39.92 — 129% above long-term mean, extreme valuation vulnerability
Margin debt at record $1.304T with 53% YoY growth — forced liquidation risk in downturn
Geopolitical escalation (Iran strikes) could trigger cascading risk-off flows
Yield curve at 0.40% — narrow but positive spread
20_day.interpretation: Moderately elevated risk. Extreme valuations and record leverage create vulnerability to shocks. The Iran strike escalation adds a fresh geopolitical catalyst. Historical 20-day base rate for 20%+ drawdown is ~1-2%; adjusted to 5% given valuations, leverage, and active geopolitical risk.

key_drivers: Buffett Indicator at 253% — all-time record, far above 'significantly overvalued' threshold
Negative equity risk premium (earnings yield 4.72% vs 10Y yield 4.53%) — stocks barely compensating vs bonds
Private credit default rates rising — systemic stress building in $2T+ shadow lending market
Margin debt YoY growth of 53.3% — historically extreme leverage expansion
Geopolitical risk: Iran strikes + ongoing Russia-Ukraine conflict could compound
Market breadth weakening with only 55% above 200DMA
60_day.interpretation: Structurally elevated risk over the 60-day horizon. The combination of extreme valuations, record leverage, rising credit stress, and active geopolitical catalysts (Iran strikes, trade policy uncertainty) creates meaningful vulnerability. Historical 60-day base rate of ~3-5% adjusted upward to 8.5% for current conditions.

crash_probability.methodology: Based on current VIX level, technical indicators, macro conditions, historical base rates, and market structure analysis.

metadata.data_source: Yahoo Finance (yfinance), FRED, CBOE, CNN Business, FINRA, GuruFocus, buffettindicator.org, weather.gov
metadata.disclaimer: This data is for informational purposes only. Not financial advice. Past performance is not indicative of future results.

vix.name: CBOE Volatility Index (VIX)
vix.interpretation: VIX at 20.61 is slightly above its long-term average of ~19.5, indicating moderately elevated implied volatility. The Iran strike selloff on June 10 pushed fear higher. VIX remains below panic thresholds (>30) but warrants monitoring.

put_call_ratio.name: CBOE Equity Put/Call Ratio
put_call_ratio.interpretation: Equity put/call ratio at 0.66 is slightly below the historical average of 0.65, but has risen sharply from 0.52 a week ago, reflecting rapidly increasing hedging demand amid the Iran-related selloff.

fear_greed_index.name: CNN Fear & Greed Index
fear_greed_index.interpretation: Fear & Greed at 27.6 is in the 'Fear' zone, a sharp decline from the 'Neutral' reading of 54 just one week ago. The rapid shift reflects the Iran strike geopolitical shock. Paradoxically, deep fear readings can be contrarian bullish signals for near-term price action.

dalio_bubble_indicator.name: Dalio-Style Bubble Indicator
dalio_bubble_indicator.interpretation: Composite score of 60.8/100 indicates 'Bubble' conditions. Extreme valuations (CAPE 39.92, Buffett 253.0%) and record leverage drive very high scores in price and leverage gauges, while fearful sentiment (F&G 27.6) and neutral RSI (52.83) pull sentiment gauges lower. The disconnect between valuation excess and near-term fear creates an unstable equilibrium — historically a precursor to sharp directional moves.

prices_vs_traditional_measures.description: How high are prices relative to traditional measures (CAPE, Buffett Indicator)?

unsustainable_conditions.description: Are prices discounting unsustainable conditions (equity risk premium)?

new_buyer_entry.description: How many new buyers have entered the market (retail participation proxy)?

bullish_sentiment.description: How broadly bullish is sentiment (Fear & Greed, RSI)?

leverage_purchases.description: Are purchases being financed by high leverage (margin debt)?

forward_purchases.description: Have buyers made extended forward purchases (capex, forward speculation)?

dalio_bubble_indicator.methodology: Rules-based proxy of Ray Dalio's 6-gauge bubble framework. Each gauge scores 0-100 using CAPE, Buffett Indicator, equity risk premium, Fear & Greed Index, RSI, and margin debt metrics. Composite is equal-weighted average.
dalio_bubble_indicator.source_note: Rules-based proxy — not Bridgewater's proprietary indicator. Inspired by Dalio's publicly described 6-gauge framework.

shiller_cape.name: Shiller CAPE Ratio (Cyclically Adjusted P/E)
shiller_cape.interpretation: CAPE at 39.92 is 129% above the long-term mean of 17.4x. While below the all-time high of 44.2 set in late 2025, this level has historically preceded below-average forward 10-year returns. Only the 2000 dot-com peak saw a comparably extreme CAPE.

buffett_indicator.name: Buffett Indicator (Total Market Cap / GDP)
buffett_indicator.interpretation: At 253.0%, the Buffett Indicator is at an all-time record — far above the 'significantly overvalued' threshold of 150%. US market cap ($80.5T) relative to GDP ($31.8T) exceeds the 2021 peak of 218.7% and the 2000 dot-com peak of 162.6%. Even globalization-adjusted (125%), valuations remain stretched.

yield_curve_10y2y.name: 10-Year minus 2-Year Treasury Spread
yield_curve_10y2y.interpretation: The 10Y-2Y spread at 0.4% is positive but well below the long-term average of 0.85%. The curve re-steepened from its 2023-2024 inversion and is now at the 2nd percentile of its 52-week range (38-74 bps). The narrow positive spread suggests the recession risk signal from the 2023-2024 inversion is still within its typical 12-24 month lag window.
yield_curve_10y2y.recession_signal: Caution — curve un-inverted ~21 months ago. Historical average lag from un-inversion to recession is 6-24 months. Currently in the watch window.

sp500_technicals.name: S&P 500 Technical Analysis
sp500_technicals.overall_interpretation: S&P 500 at 7386.65 is 2.9% below its all-time high of 7609.78. The golden cross (50DMA > 200DMA) remains intact with price above both moving averages. RSI at 52.83 is neutral. The overall technical picture is moderately constructive despite the recent Iran-related selloff.

price_vs_50dma.interpretation: Price is 2.67% above the 50-day MA (7195), indicating the short-term uptrend remains intact.

price_vs_200dma.interpretation: Price is 7.55% above the 200-day MA (6868), confirming the long-term uptrend.

golden_cross.interpretation: 50-day MA (7195) is above 200-day MA (6868). Golden cross is active — historically a bullish long-term signal.

rsi_assessment.interpretation: RSI at 52.83 is in the neutral zone (30-70). No overbought or oversold extremes — market has room to move in either direction.

drawdown_assessment.interpretation: Current drawdown of -2.93% from ATH (7609.78) is a mild pullback. Drawdowns of 5-10% are normal corrections; current level does not signal systemic stress.

sp500_above_50dma.name: S&P 500 Stocks Above 50-Day Moving Average
sp500_above_50dma.interpretation: 50% of S&P 500 stocks are above their 50-day moving average, down from higher levels. This indicates weakening intermediate-term breadth and increasing selectivity in the rally.

sp500_above_200dma.name: S&P 500 Stocks Above 200-Day Moving Average (S5TH)
sp500_above_200dma.interpretation: 55% of S&P 500 stocks are above their 200-day moving average. Moderate long-term breadth suggests the uptrend is supported but not broad-based. Energy (87%) leads while Consumer Defensive (40%) lags.

advance_decline_ratio.name: NYSE Advance/Decline Ratio
advance_decline_ratio.interpretation: A/D ratio at 0.81 (277 advancing vs 224 declining as of last session) indicates slightly more advancing than declining issues but below the normal range of 0.8-1.5. This borderline reading suggests a market in transition.

overall_breadth_assessment.interpretation: Market breadth is weakening from recent peaks. The narrowing participation — with only 50% above the 50DMA and 55% above the 200DMA — suggests increasing concentration risk. Sector divergence (Energy 87% vs Consumer Defensive 40%) indicates rotation rather than broad decline.

finra_margin_debt.name: FINRA Margin Debt
finra_margin_debt.interpretation: FINRA margin debt reached $1.304 trillion in April 2026, a new all-time high. Year-over-year growth of 53.3% is historically extreme — exceeding the pace seen before both the 2000 and 2008 crashes. This level of leveraged positioning creates significant forced-liquidation risk in any sustained downturn.
risks: Record absolute level creates unprecedented forced-liquidation risk in a selloff
53% YoY growth exceeds pre-2000 and pre-2008 leverage expansion rates
Margin calls in a -10% correction could trigger cascading liquidations across correlated positions
finra_margin_debt.regulatory_note: FINRA Regulation 4210 sets minimum margin requirements at 25% maintenance. Brokers may impose higher house requirements, but rapid market declines can still trigger forced selling.

overview.interpretation: The private credit market has grown to an estimated $2.1 trillion in AUM, up from ~$1.2T in 2020. Rapid growth, declining underwriting standards, and rising default rates create systemic risk through bank interconnectedness and liquidity mismatches.

default_rates.name: Default Rate
default_rates.interpretation: Private credit default rates have risen to approximately 2.8% in early 2026, up from ~1.8% in late 2025. Including liability management exercises (LMEs), effective defaults approach 4.5%. The trend is accelerating, particularly in software and small-issuer segments.

borrower_health.name: Borrower Health
borrower_health.interpretation: An estimated 45% of private credit borrowers are generating negative free cash flow, up from 38% in 2021. Interest coverage ratios have compressed as base rates remain elevated, straining borrower ability to service floating-rate debt.

pik_usage.name: PIK Interest Usage
pik_usage.interpretation: Payment-in-kind (PIK) interest usage continues to rise as borrowers negotiate deferred cash payments. PIK toggle features, originally designed for temporary relief, are increasingly used as ongoing survival mechanisms.
pik_usage.warning_threshold: PIK usage > 15% of portfolio indicates stress
pik_usage.trend: Increasing — estimated 12-15% of private credit deals now include PIK components
mechanisms: PIK toggle — borrower can elect to pay interest in additional debt rather than cash
PIK step-up — PIK interest rate increases over time to incentivize eventual cash payment
PIK holdco — interest is paid in kind at a holding company level, masking subsidiary stress

liquidity_risk.name: Liquidity Risk
liquidity_risk.interpretation: Liquidity mismatches are growing as semi-liquid evergreen funds accumulate $250B+ in AUM. Redemption gates have been triggered at several funds, and the illiquidity premium is compressing as competition increases. A stress event could trigger a rush for exits in structures not designed for rapid liquidation.
gated_funds: Multiple evergreen vehicles have imposed quarterly redemption gates of 5-10%
Several large BDCs trading at 8-12% discounts to NAV
Secondary market for private credit LP interests showing wider bid-ask spreads
risks: Redemption queues in semi-liquid funds could cascade if gates are hit simultaneously
NAV markdowns lag realized losses by 1-2 quarters, creating cliff risk
Insurance company allocations to private credit create interconnected liquidity risk

bank_interconnectedness.name: Bank Exposure to Private Credit
bank_interconnectedness.interpretation: Major banks have deep exposure to private credit through direct lending, warehouse lines, fund financing, and co-investment. Total NDFI (non-depository financial institution) exposure exceeds $3.5 trillion across the banking system, up from $2.1T in 2010. Regulatory scrutiny is increasing but gaps remain.
bank_interconnectedness.regulatory_response: Federal Reserve and OCC have increased examination focus on NDFI exposures; Basel III endgame implementation delayed

jpmorgan.private_credit: $60B direct + warehouse lines to PC funds

citigroup.private_credit_warehouse: $35B in warehouse facilities for PC managers

goldman_sachs.other_collateral_lending: $95B in fund financing and prime brokerage to PC
goldman_sachs.target_pc_platform: $150B AUM target for private credit platform by 2027

shadow_defaults.name: Shadow Defaults
shadow_defaults.description: Distressed debt restructurings occurring outside formal bankruptcy — including amendments, extensions, liability management exercises (LMEs), and distressed exchanges — that mask true default rates in private credit portfolios.
shadow_defaults.interpretation: When including shadow defaults (LMEs, amend-and-extends, distressed exchanges), the effective default rate in private credit approximately doubles from the reported ~2.8% to an estimated 6-7%. These mechanisms defer losses rather than resolve them, building latent credit risk in portfolios.
mechanisms: Amend-and-extend — maturity pushed out with covenant relief, deferring but not resolving stress
Liability management exercises (LMEs) — debt restructured without formal default, often stripping collateral from existing lenders
Distressed exchanges — debt swapped at below par value, effectively a default in economics but not in name

sectors.name: Software & Technology
sectors.risk: High — many SaaS companies with negative FCF and high leverage

sectors.name: Healthcare Services
sectors.risk: Elevated — reimbursement pressure and labor cost inflation

sectors.name: Automotive & Industrial
sectors.risk: High — cyclical downturn + EV transition costs

sectors.name: Retail & Consumer
sectors.risk: Elevated — consumer spending deceleration and inventory buildup

systemic_risk_assessment.interpretation: Private credit's rapid growth, declining underwriting standards, and increasing bank interconnectedness create a systemically important risk pocket. While structural features (longer lock-ups, floating rates) differ from 2008 mortgage structures, the opacity, leverage, and interconnectedness rhyme with pre-crisis patterns.
systemic_risk_assessment.comparison_to_2008: Unlike 2008 MBS: no widespread securitization of PC loans (yet), longer lock-up periods reduce run risk, and borrowers are primarily corporate (not consumer). However: similar opacity in asset quality, comparable leverage ratios, growing bank exposure, and the same 'this time is different' narrative.
crash_transmission_channels: Bank warehouse line pulls triggering forced sales of PC portfolios at distressed prices
Insurance company liquidity strain from PC allocation markdowns affecting policyholder claims
BDC NAV collapses triggering retail investor panic and secondary market contagion
Cross-default clauses in leveraged loan agreements creating cascading failures across portfolios
mitigating_factors: Longer lock-up periods in PE-style vehicles reduce immediate run risk
Floating-rate structures mean less duration risk vs fixed-rate MBS
Regulatory awareness is higher — Fed and OCC actively monitoring NDFI exposures
Most private credit is senior secured, providing better recovery rates than unsecured bonds
regulatory_gaps: No consolidated reporting requirement for private credit fund leverage
Valuation methodology varies widely across managers — no standardized mark-to-market requirement
Insurance company PC allocations are regulated at the state level with inconsistent standards
Shadow default mechanisms (LMEs, amend-and-extend) are not captured in standard default statistics

near_term_stress.description: VIX at 20.6 and Fear & Greed at 27.6 reflect elevated near-term stress from Iran strike escalation. The rapid sentiment shift from Neutral to Fear zone within one week is notable, but deep fear often precedes short-term bounces.

valuation_vulnerability.description: CAPE at 39.9x (129% above mean), Buffett Indicator at 253% (all-time record), and negative equity risk premium create extreme valuation vulnerability. Any catalyst that reprices growth expectations could trigger outsized declines.

technical_regime.description: Golden cross intact, price above both 50 and 200-day MAs, RSI neutral at 52.8. Technical structure is constructive despite the -2.9% drawdown from ATH. No death cross or major support break.

market_structure.description: Record margin debt ($1.3T, +53% YoY), narrowing market breadth (50% above 50DMA), and growing private credit stress create structural fragility. Forced liquidation risk from leveraged positions is the primary structural concern.

macro_environment.description: 10Y yield at 4.53%, yield curve positive but narrow at 0.40%. Geopolitical risk elevated (Iran strikes, Russia-Ukraine). The economy continues to grow but the post-inversion recession watch window remains active.

key_warnings: Buffett Indicator at all-time record 253% — historically extreme market cap relative to GDP
FINRA margin debt at $1.3T with 53% YoY growth — unprecedented leverage expansion
Iran strike escalation — potential for oil price spike and broader geopolitical contagion
Private credit default rates rising while shadow defaults mask true deterioration
Equity risk premium near zero — stocks offering minimal compensation vs risk-free bonds
mitigating_factors: Golden cross and price above key moving averages — technical uptrend intact
Fear & Greed at 27.6 — contrarian buy signal from deep fear territory
No yield curve inversion — recession probability model is cautious, not alarming
Corporate earnings growth continues to be supported by AI capital expenditure cycle
Fed has room to cut rates if needed — currently accommodative stance possible

base_case.outlook: Shallow consolidation (-5 to -10%) followed by recovery. Iran tensions de-escalate or are priced in within 1-2 weeks. AI spending cycle and earnings momentum resume driving markets higher by year-end.
triggers: Diplomatic resolution or containment of Iran situation
Continued strong earnings from tech/AI leaders

correction_case.outlook: 10-20% correction driven by geopolitical escalation and/or valuation compression. Iran conflict broadens, oil prices spike above $100, or earnings disappoint. Recovery takes 3-6 months.
triggers: Iran conflict escalation beyond initial strikes
Oil price spike above $100/bbl triggering recession fears
Margin call cascade from leveraged positions

crash_case.outlook: 20%+ crash triggered by compounding catalysts — geopolitical shock amplified by margin liquidation and credit contagion. The combination of extreme valuations, record leverage, and geopolitical uncertainty creates crash potential if multiple risk factors align.
triggers: Full-scale Middle East conflict disrupting oil supply
Simultaneous margin call cascade and private credit fund runs
Unexpected earnings collapse in AI/tech sector
Sovereign debt crisis or major bank failure

recommendation_framework.conservative_investors: Maintain elevated cash allocation (15-25%). Consider Treasury bills yielding 4.5%+ as a compelling alternative to stretched equity valuations. Reduce exposure to leveraged and speculative positions.
recommendation_framework.moderate_investors: Maintain diversified allocation with tilt toward quality and value. Consider protective puts on concentrated equity positions. Dollar-cost averaging rather than lump-sum deployment given valuation extremes.
recommendation_framework.aggressive_investors: Current fear readings present potential opportunities in high-quality names sold off indiscriminately. However, be aware of extreme leverage in the system — position sizing and stop losses are critical. Avoid margin exposure.

overall_assessment.historical_context: Current conditions most closely resemble late 1999-early 2000 in terms of valuation extremes (CAPE and Buffett Indicator) combined with the geopolitical uncertainty of early 2020 (COVID shock). The key difference is the AI productivity narrative which, if sustained, could justify higher-than-historical multiples — but narratives rarely prevent corrections when leverage unwinds.
overall_assessment.disclaimer: This assessment is for informational purposes only and does not constitute financial advice. Market conditions can change rapidly. Past indicator readings and historical patterns are not reliable predictors of future market behavior.
